text:
Structome-AlignViewer: On Confidence 1 Assessment in Structure-Aware Alignments 2

- Ashar J. Malik 1,2,3,* , Siying Mao 1,2 , Philip Hugenholtz 1,2 , and David B. Ascher 1,2,3,* 3
- 1 School of Chemistry and Molecular Biosciences, The University of Queensland, 4
- Brisbane, Australia 5
- 2 Australian Centre for Ecogenomics, The University of Queensland, Brisbane, 6
- Australia 7
- 3 Computational Biology and Clinical Informatics, Baker Heart and Diabetes 8
- Institute, Melbourne, Victoria, Australia 9
* Correspondence to Ashar J. Malik: ashar.malik@uq.edu.au, David B. Ascher: 10
- d.ascher@uq.edu.au 11

12

Abstract

Protein structure-based comparison provides a framework for uncovering deep evolu13 tionary relationships that can escape conventional sequence-based approaches. En14 coding three-dimensional protein structures using a simplified structure-aware al15 phabet, can lead to compact, comparable strings that retain key spatial relation16 ships. Although this enables comparison, structure-aware alignments can experi17 ence misaligned regions, particularly when comparing proteins with substantial di18 vergence in fold architecture. To address this, a web-based resource, Structome19 AlignViewer, is introduced in this work for evaluating the quality of structure-aware 20 alignments through both spatial mapping of alignment columns to protein struc21 tures and quantitative confidence scoring. Confidence is computed from pairwise 22 structural substitutions between adjacent inputs and normalized within each align23 ment to highlight relatively well-supported columns. To provide broader context, 24 thousands of alignments from established structural classification systems were anal25 ysed, allowing for an empirical comparative statistic to be derived to assess align26 ment quality. Option to exclude gap-rich regions enable users to refine alignments 27 and focus on conserved structural cores. This approach provides an interpretable 28 method for assessing structural alignment quality and supports more robust com29

parative and evolutionary analyses. Structome-AlignViewer is freely available 30 https://biosig.lab.uq.edu.au/structome_alignviewer/ . 31

Keywords 32

- Structure-based alignment; Protein structure comparison, Alignment confidence scor33
- 34

ing, Deep structural homology, Structural phylogenetics

Introduction 35

Determining homology among biological macromolecules has long been central to 36 unravelling the history of life on Earth. By identifying relationships among genes 37 and proteins, homology inference provides the scaffolding upon which evolutionary 38 biology, comparative genomics, and structural bioinformatics are built. 39

Traditionally, molecular sequence data have served as the primary source for de40 tecting evolutionary relationships [1]. While comparative sequence-based analysis has 41 driven decades of discovery, it faces a fundamental limitation: as sequences diverge 42 over deep evolutionary time, the homology signal progressively erodes, making it in43 creasingly difficult to discern signal from noise. This loss of detectable similarity 44 defines the so-called 'twilight zone' [2] of sequence comparison, beyond which many 45 evolutionary relationships are missed. 46

47

Protein structure, in contrast, tends to be more robust to changes in the underly-

- ing amino acid sequence [3, 4, 5]. It preserves key aspects of molecular function and 48
- folding architecture even as mutations accumulate and sequence identity diminishes. 49
- This robustness has made structural data an appealing alternative for recovering 50
- deeper evolutionary signals [5, 6]. A recent advance in defining a structural alpha51
- bet-the introduction of 3Di states used by Foldseek [7]-has opened a new frontier. 52

53

By converting protein structures into 3Di sequences, classical alignment and phyloge-

54

netic tools can be applied to this structure-aware alphabet, effectively extending the

- temporal reach of evolutionary inference [8, 9]. 55

This structure-informed evolutionary analysis generally follows a three-step pro56 cess [8]: (1) compile a dataset of proteins [10, 11], (2) align their structure-aware 57

- at

sequences, and (3) build a phylogenetic tree from the resulting alignment [8]. As 58 promising as this approach is, the novelty of the method raises important open ques59 tions about how best to apply and interpret the inference. 60

One key aspect, of the many, that warrants a deeper exploration is the quality of 61 the alignment itself where even slight column misplacements can distort phylogenetic 62 inference by skewing comparative estimates and resulting tree topologies [12, 13, 63 14]. While the structure-aware alignment strategy successfully incorporates three64 dimensional context, at present users lack a straightforward way to (i) visually map 65 regions back to the actual structures for manual review and (ii) assess per-column 66 reliability. Consequently, it can be difficult to refine this new class of alignments 67 and ensure that downstream maximum likelihood or other statistical phylogenetic 68 methods receive correct inputs. 69

In this work, Structome-AlignViewer, an interactive, web-based platform is intro70 duced that addresses this challenge by providing a fast and user-friendly environment 71 to compute, inspect, and assess the quality of structure-aware alignments. The plat72 form not only links alignment columns directly to 3D structures through an interactive 73 and user-friendly interface, but also incorporates a statistical framework to quantify 74 both per-column and overall alignment confidence. To contextualise these confidence 75 values, an empirical alignment analysis across structurally homologous groups defined 76 in SCOP [15, 16] and CATH [17] was carried out, yielding a background distribution 77 of confidence scores. This distribution serves as a reference for assessing the signifi78 cance of average confidence values in user-generated alignments. 79

Overall, this resource is intended to provide more transparency for users carrying 80 out structure-based phylogenetic analysis, and hence is intended to be an important 81 advance in this area. 82

Methods 83

Structure-aware Sequence Generation 84

This work used Foldseek to generate structure-aware sequences of proteins using their 85 respective 3D structures. Foldseek assigns each amino acid in the protein structure a 86

- structure-aware character based on the tertiary interactions made by the respective 87
- residue. This results in a sequence of structure-aware characters, which collectively 88
- 89

are referred to as a structure-aware sequence in this work.

Aligning Structure-aware Sequences 90

- The alignment of structure-aware sequences was carried out using ClustalW2 [18], ap91 plying a conventional multiple sequence alignment algorithm to the structure-aware 92 representation. A custom substitution matrix [7], included with Foldseek, was pro93 vided as input to ClustalW2, which is necessary since a distinction must be made 94 between amino acids and structure-aware characters. Furthermore, alignments were 95 performed in the 'slow' mode to maximize alignment quality. 96

Per-Column Confidence Metric 97

98

99

100

101

103

104

105

107

- N is the number of sequences in the alignment,
- r i and r i +1 are the structure-aware characters from adjacent sequences at column j ,
- S( r i , r i +1 ) is the substitution score for that pair, obtained from the Foldseek substitution matrix.

Adjacent pairs involving gaps are excluded from analysis. The resulting raw col108 umn scores capture local structural coherence among neighbouring sequences. 109

To assess alignment quality, a per-column confidence score C j is computed for each alignment column j based on the sum of substitution scores between adjacent sequences in the alignment (see supplementary data for examples). For each column, all adjacent sequence pairs are considered:

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

To normalize these values within each alignment, min-max scaling is applied across 110 all columns: 111

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

where C min and C max are the minimum and maximum raw confidence scores across 112 all columns in the alignment. The resulting normalized score ˆ C j lies in the range 113 [0 , 1] and reflects the strength of structural agreement for each column relative to the 114 weakest and strongest-aligned positions in the same alignment. 115

An average of all normalized column scores is also computed per alignment: 116

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

where L is the total number of columns. This serves as a representative measure 117 of overall alignment confidence. 118

Interpretation of the Confidence Metric 119

To interpret confidence values statistically, thousands of alignments of SCOP and 120 CATH groupings were used. These alignments have previously been used in other 121 work [9]. Briefly, at the family level in SCOP and the homology level in CATH, protein 122 structures were retrieved, and domain regions were extracted based on annotations 123 from the respective classification databases. As described above, these structures 124 were converted into structure-aware sequences and alignments were generated using 125 the ClustalW2 method. 126

Two thresholds were applied to filter alignments for analysis: 127

- alignments comprising ≥ 10 structures, and 128
- alignments of length ≥ 100 residues. 129

This resulted in 2,089 SCOP alignments and 1,931 CATH alignments. Confidence 130 scores were calculated for each alignment, and the corresponding means and standard 131 deviations were determined for both classification systems. 132

Z-scores reported on the results page on Structome-AlignViewer are derived from 133 these distributions and computed separately for SCOP and CATH. The average of 134 the two Z-scores ( ˆ C avg ) is used to assign a qualitative confidence category for each 135 alignment (see Table 1). This approach offers users a way to interpret alignment 136 quality in a statistically meaningful way. 137

Table 1: Interpretation of Z-score-based alignment confidence levels, based on statistical rarity.

| Z-score Range                                                                   | Interpretation                                                                                                                         |
|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| ˆ C avg ≥ 2 1 ≤ ˆ C avg < 2 - 1 < ˆ C avg < 1 - 2 < ˆ C avg ≤ - 1 ˆ C avg ≤ - 2 | Statistically Rare Alignment Above Average, Less Common Common, Expected Range Below Average, Less Common Statistically Rare Alignment |

Implementation of the Web Interface 138

139

140

141

142

143

144

The Structome-AlignViewer resource is implemented using Flask, Docker and Nginx. On the landing page of this resource, users can provide a delimited list of RCSB PDB accessions, including chain identifiers, which if valid are automatically converted into structure-aware sequences and subsequently aligned, and analysed. All structures are downloaded from RCSB PDB at runtime. Given compute limitations, a user is limited to 50 input structures per analysis.

145

146

147

148

149

150

151

152

153

The result page uses the Mol ∗ [19] viewer to display protein structures. The feature viewer shows the alignment, in which columns are clickable, allowing users to instantly view the corresponding column in the structure selected. A drop-down menu allows users to toggle between different structures in the alignment. Furthermore, confidence scores are also encoded as B-factors in the rendered structures, enabling fast, intuitive colour-based visualization. Low confidence values approaching '0' appear towards the blue end of the spectrum and high confidence values approaching '1' appear towards the red end of the colour scale. Additionally users can obtain publication quality images from the native image export utility in Mol ∗ .

155

Structome-AlignViewer also implements a Gblocks-style trimming option [20], which removes alignment columns with > 50% gap content. Raw structure-aware sequences for all structures along with both full and trimmed alignments and the dendrogram, which is also displayed on the results page, are available for download.

Biopython is used both on Structome-AlignViewer and for other analysis included in this work to parse and analyse alignments and load confidence values as B-factors in the respective protein structures for subsequent visualization in the Mol ∗ viewer.

Results

Background Confidence Distributions from SCOP and CATH

To contextualize the confidence scores computed for user-submitted alignments, background confidence scores distributions from curated SCOP and CATH alignments were determined. These distributions serve as the statistical reference against which Z-scores are calculated. The violin plots shown in Figure 1 show these distributions for both SCOP and CATH datasets, comparing the full alignments (blue) to their trimmed counterparts (orange).

As shown, trimming columns with high gap content results in a modest increase in average confidence since the gapped columns do not contribute to the score and the trimming results in reduced lengths, thus increasing the score. The trimmed alignments can therefore be interpreted as a refined subset, potentially more informative and suitable for downstream applications.

Relationship Between Confidence and Gap Fraction

To further understand the drivers of alignment confidence, the relationship between gap fraction (i.e., proportion of gaps in the alignment) and the average confidence score was examined. As expected, a strong negative correlation was observed: alignments with higher gap content tend to exhibit lower average confidence. Figures 2 and 3 show this inverse relationship for SCOP and CATH alignments, respectively.

These results reinforce the assumption that gaps often reflect regions of uncertainty or structural divergence. A high gap fraction indicates that many sites lack Confidence (Avg)

0.8

0.6 -

0.4-

0.2- Full Trimmed consistently aligned counterparts-whether due to flexible/disordered segments or 182 failure to identify a common structural core. Such columns reduce alignment relia183 bility and introduce noise in downstream analyses. Thus, removing gap-rich columns 184 can reduce poorly supported positions and enrich the alignment. 185

Figure 1: Distribution of average confidence scores for SCOP and CATH alignments. Each dataset is shown for both full alignments and their trimmed versions (Gblocks-style trimming with > 50% gap removal).

Building on this, the gap-confidence relationship also sets the stage for confidence186 guided pruning strategies, in which columns with poor structural support or excessive 187 gaps are systematically omitted. Refined alignments generated by this approach 188 are well suited for non-parametric bootstrapping or any phylogenetic pipeline where 189 reducing alignment noise is crucial. Thus, gap fraction emerges not merely as a 190 technical artifact but as a biologically meaningful indicator of alignment quality-and, 191 by extension, a practical filter for enhancing it. 192

Figure 2: Correlation between gap fraction and average confidence score for SCOP alignments. Each point represents a single alignment, and a linear regression line (with 95% confidence interval) is overlaid in red. A strong negative correlation is observed (r= -0 . 76).

Discussion

Assessing alignments using the confidence metric

Any structural alignment carried out for the purpose of determining homology, aims to identify equivalent positions across proteins compared. These equivalent positions then form the backbone of evolutionary comparison, delineating how folds are conserved, diverge, or adapt across the course of their respective evolutionary trajectories. Simply encoding 3D structures into structure-aware sequences before comparison does not necessarily guarantee a better alignment. As proteins diverge, segments may be introduced, which although part of a common fold may lack clear structural correspondence across the proteins, becoming especially pronounced when aligning distantly related proteins.

To determine if the alignment is reliable, the resource presented in this work, Structome-AlignViewer, offers two complementary layers of inspection. First, it enables users to directly map alignment columns back onto 3D structures which are rendered using the Mol ∗ protein structure viewer. This spatial mapping helps assess whether incompatible structure-aware characters have been aligned. Secondly, the

Figure 3: Correlation between gap fraction and average confidence score for CATH alignments. Each point represents a single alignment, and a linear regression line (with 95% confidence interval) is overlaid in red. Similar to SCOP, a strong negative correlation is observed (r= -0 . 79).

confidence score brings statistical context and provides a way to evaluate alignment 209 quality against the background alignments generated from curated SCOP and CATH 210 datasets. 211

212

213

214

215

216

217

The confidence metric derived per alignment originates from the same substitution matrix used during the alignment process. While the alignment aims to optimally determine overlap of structure-aware sequences, the confidence score adds an additional dimension, on a per-column basis, to the resulting alignment. While the scoring and alignment frameworks differ mathematically, they are unified in vocabulary and logic, making the confidence score a natural extension of the alignment itself.

Additionally, confidence values are scaled to highlight the best and worst aligned 218 columns and thus reflect relative structural consistency rather than an absolute qual219 ity metric. Within each column, both identical and mismatched structure-aware 220 characters are scored according to the structural alphabet's substitution matrix. The 221 normalization of all raw scores to a [0,1] range based on the alignment's highest and 222 lowest performing columns, not only captures whether residues agree but also how 223 strongly they agree. As a result, columns that outperform the alignment's average 224

225

226

227

228

229

230

231

232

233

234

235

236

237

238

248

become visibly more trustworthy, highlighting where the alignment is particularly well supported.

Furthermore, to add context to user-generated structure-aware sequence alignments, the average normalized confidence score is computed and compared to the distributions of the same metric derived from thousands of SCOP and CATH alignments. Although each alignment is normalized independently, these averages form meaningful empirical distributions. The resulting Z-scores allow users to contextualise their alignment within the known homologous families. While this score does not predict phylogenetic correctness, it offers a principled and statistically grounded way to assess alignment quality, and can help guide decisions about trimming, weighting, or downstream analysis.

Limitations of the confidence metric

It must be emphasized that alignment confidence is not equivalent to phylogenetic confidence. While a reliable alignment is essential for downstream tree construction, high alignment confidence does not imply correctness of the inferred topology. Many other factors-substitution model choice, rate heterogeneity, long-branch attraction, and sampling-can influence phylogenetic inference independently of alignment quality.

The purpose of Structome-AlignViewer is to provide users with a clearer view of how well their alignment represents conserved structural signals. Properly interpreted, confidence scores can guide alignment refinement without overextending their meaning whereas over-interpreting the score beyond its intended scope risks conflating alignment quality with evolutionary truth-a distinction that remains critical.

Conclusion

Structome-AlignViewer, the resource introduced in this work, provides a robust frame249 work for users to confidently assess structure-aware sequence alignments. It achieves 250 this using two complementary methods. It allows visual inspection of alignment 251 columns and additionally associates a confidence score with these columns. This 252

combination allows users to assess the overall reliability of an alignment and identify 253 regions that are well aligned across the protein structures compared. Additionally, 254 to ensure consistency, the confidence score is calculated using the same substitution 255 matrix employed for generating the alignment and is further normalised to facili256 tate comparisons both within a single alignment, and for computing averages which 257 are then scored against extensive SCOP and CATH-derived distributions to provide 258 valuable context. 259

260

261

262

263

264

265

While the confidence score, developed in this work, is not intended to be used towards assessing phylogenetic accuracy, it does open valuable avenues for further refinement. One immediate application is alignment pruning-removing poorly supported columns prior to tree inference. For example, in the case of non-parametric bootstrapping eliminating noisy positions could enhance signal-to-noise ratio and lead to reduced uncertainty in inferred trees.

266

267

268

269

As structure-based phylogenetics matures, Structome-AlignViewer will be instrumental in enhancing the reliability of structural comparisons carried out using structureaware sequences and the evolutionary conclusions drawn from them.

Availability

Structome-AlignViewer is freely available as a web application at: 270 https://biosig.lab.uq.edu.au/structome_alignviewer/ 271

272

metadata.preprint_title: Structome-AlignViewer: On Confidence Assessment in Structure-Aware Alignments

pub_metadata.preprint_title: Structome-AlignViewer: On Confidence Assessment in Structure-Aware Alignments
pub_metadata.preprint_authors: Malik, A. J.; Mao, S.; Hugenholtz, P. J.; Ascher, D. B.
pub_metadata.preprint_abstract: Protein structure-based comparison provides a framework for uncovering deep evolutionary relationships that can escape conventional sequence-based approaches. Encoding three-dimensional protein structures using a simplified structure-aware alphabet, can lead to compact, comparable strings that retain key spatial relationships. Although this enables comparison, structure-aware alignments can experience misaligned regions, particularly when comparing proteins with substantial divergence in fold architecture. To address this, a web-based resource, Structome-AlignViewer, is introduced in this work for evaluating the quality of structure-aware alignments through both spatial mapping of alignment columns to protein structures and quantitative confidence scoring. Confidence is computed from pairwise structural substitutions between adjacent inputs and normalized within each alignment to highlight relatively well-supported columns. To provide broader context, thousands of alignments from established structural classification systems were analysed, allowing for an empirical comparative statistic to be derived to assess alignment quality. Option to exclude gap-rich regions enable users to refine alignments and focus on conserved structural cores. This approach provides an interpretable method for assessing structural alignment quality and supports more robust comparative and evolutionary analyses. Structome-AlignViewer is freely available at https://biosig.lab.uq.edu.au/structome_alignviewer/.
pub_metadata.preprint_author_corresponding_institutio: School of Chemistry & Molecular Biosciences, University of Queensland
